A Data Story of Spotify’s 2023 Hits

Salome Shunamon

March 10, 2026

The Soundtrack of 2023

Elgiriyewithana, Nidula. “Most Streamed Spotify Songs 2023.” Kaggle, 2023. Hottest Spotify Hits. Accessed March 10, 2026. https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/nelgiriyewithana/top-spotify-songs-2023

This dataset, compiled by Nidula Elgiriyewithana in 2023, provides a comprehensive look at the most streamed songs on Spotify. It includes the track information, artist information, popularity on various platforms and the musical DNA that makes a song a global phenomenon according to Spotify metrics.

Tempo Takeover

Tempo measured in Beats Per Minute (BPM) is the heartbeat of a song. It informs us to bob our heads slowly or jump out of our chairs. It can even dictate how we should dance based on trends. We will look at the distribution of tempos across the most streamed songs to find the most popular tempo for 2023.

The distribution is slightly skewed right meaning there’s a longer tail of songs with very high BPMs. However, there is a clear peak in the 115-125 BPM range. The median tempo is 121 BPM, a speed that is fast enough to feel energetic but not too fast to be considered a workout. This is the tempo of a steady skipping. It appears that for a song to capture the world’s attention in 2023, it needed to be ~120 BPM.

Energy vs. Mood

A song’s impact isn’t just about its speed. It’s about its feeling too and Spotify provides two key audio features to describe this: Energy (a measure of intensity and activity) and Valence (a measure of musical happiness). Do these two elements interact in the most successful songs?

This scatter plot offers a visual of what makes a song exciting. The color gradient shows that many of the most streamed tracks are not only energetic but also are dense in the upper right quadrant where both energy and valence are high. This is where we find upbeat and good feeling songs. The size of the bubbles show the total streams so that verifies this idea. The largest bubble are the biggest hits and are mostly found in this zone. This suggests that even though sad or low energy songs can still be popular, its the songs that make you want to get up and feel good that dominate the charts.

Song Key and Dancability

Some keys are traditionally described as happier while others feel dark or sad. But does the choice of key actually influence a measurable danceability? By comparing danceability scores across different musical keys, can we see if certain keys naturally inform more movement inclined music?

Major keys generally support higher danceability than minor keys. For most major keys the median danceability is slightly higher than minor keys. However, D Major songs are clear exceptions, yet they have a few of the highest maximum values. This tells me that while songs in these keys are typically less danceable, they can produce some hit danceable tracks when other musical elements are involved. The data ultimately suggests that key alone does not likely determine a song’s danceability much though. The wide ranges within each suggest much more than just the primary key plays a role in dancability.

Summary

This analysis of Spotify’s most streamed songs of 2023 reveals that hit songs consistently group around a 121 BPM tempo, creating an energetic yet accessible rhythm for listeners. While major keys generally support higher danceability than minor keys, the data shows that key alone does not determine a song’s danceability. Instead, it’s more likely that a combination of song qualities determine what is a hit song. Finally, analyzing song data may be useful in the future for determining some factors that may influence the design of hit songs though backwards engineering songs based on qualities of other songs that have become very popular.
